questions.question: What is the fundamental cause of myopia?
questions.explanation: Myopia is defined as a refractive error caused by excess dioptric power in the eye, which causes the image to form in front of the retina.

questions.question: What happens to the image in a myopic eye?
options: Forms in front of the retina
questions.explanation: In myopia, due to excess dioptric power, light rays converge before reaching the retina, forming the image in front of it.

questions.question: What is the standard optical correction for myopia?
questions.explanation: Myopia is corrected with divergent (concave) lenses that reduce excess dioptric power and shift the image toward the retina.

questions.question: What anatomical alteration is frequently associated with myopia?
options: Increased axial length of the eye
questions.explanation: Axial myopia, the most common form, is due to an increase in the anteroposterior length of the eyeball, which contributes to excess dioptric power.

questions.question: What type of refractive error is myopia?
questions.explanation: Simple myopia is a refractive error caused by an excess of dioptric power, differentiating from hyperopia (deficit) and astigmatism (corneal irregularity).

questions.question: What is the main retinal complication associated with high myopia?
questions.explanation: High myopia, due to stretching of the eyeball, predisposes to retinal detachment due to vitreous traction and the formation of retinal tears.

questions.question: What is the main cause of axial myopia?
questions.explanation: Axial myopia is caused by excessive elongation of the eyeball, which increases the distance between the cornea and the retina, causing the image to form in front of it.

questions.question: What characterizes refractive myopia due to curvature?
questions.explanation: In refractive myopia due to curvature, the cornea or lens has an excessive curvature, which increases the dioptric power and diverts the focus in front of the retina.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism underlies index myopia?
options: Increase in the refractive index of the lens
questions.explanation: Index myopia is due to an increase in the refractive index of the lens, as occurs in incipient cataracts, which diverts the focus in front of the retina without altering the axial length.

questions.question: Which of the following is a typical example of refractive myopia?
questions.explanation: Keratoconus produces excessive corneal curvature, which generates refractive myopia due to curvature, unlike other causes that do not directly alter the dioptric power of the cornea or lens.

questions.question: What test confirms axial myopia as a cause of ametropia?
questions.explanation: Ocular biometry measures the axial length of the eyeball, allowing axial (long axis) myopia to be differentiated from refractive myopia (altered curvature or index) objectively.

questions.question: Which of the following options corresponds to a refractive treatment for axial myopia?
questions.explanation: LASIK modifies the corneal curvature to compensate for excess power, being an effective refractive surgical option in axial myopia, although it does not correct the length of the eye.

questions.question: What anatomical alteration is the main cause of axial myopia?
options: Flattening of the curvature of the lens
Decreased depth of the anterior chamber
questions.explanation: Axial myopia is caused by an elongation of the anteroposterior axis of the eyeball, which causes the image to be formed in front of the retina.

questions.question: What is the normal length of the anteroposterior axis of the adult eyeball?
questions.explanation: The normal anteroposterior axis of the eyeball measures approximately 22-24 mm. Higher values ​​indicate axial myopia.

questions.question: What type of myopia is associated with lengthening of the anteroposterior axis?
questions.explanation: Axial myopia is caused by an increase in the length of the anteroposterior axis of the eyeball, while other types have corneal or lens origin.

questions.question: What is the main consequence of elongation of the anteroposterior axis in myopia?
options: Image focused in front of the retina
questions.explanation: The lengthening of the anteroposterior axis causes light rays to converge before reaching the retina, forming a blurred image of distant objects.

questions.question: What risk factor is most related to the development of axial myopia?
questions.explanation: Prolonged reading and close work during childhood are known risk factors for elongation of the anteroposterior axis and the development of axial myopia.

questions.question: What is the first-line optical treatment for axial myopia?
questions.explanation: Concave spherical (negative) lenses diverge light rays, compensating for the excess refractive power caused by the lengthening of the anteroposterior axis in axial myopia.

questions.question: What is the main symptom of uncorrected myopia?
questions.explanation: Myopia is characterized by difficulty focusing on distant objects because the image is formed in front of the retina, causing blurred distance vision.

questions.question: What behavior typically reflects adaptation to uncorrected myopia?
questions.explanation: Squinting is a compensatory mechanism that reduces the diameter of the eyelid opening, temporarily improving visual acuity by reducing light dispersion.

questions.question: Which of the following headaches is most frequently associated with myopia?
questions.explanation: Frontal tension headache is common in uncorrected myopia, since the accommodative effort and visual fatigue when trying to focus on distant objects generate tension in the pericranial muscles.

questions.question: What ocular structure is mainly responsible for axial myopia?
questions.explanation: In axial myopia, the eyeball is longer than normal in its anteroposterior axis, which causes light rays to converge before reaching the retina, generating blurred distance vision.

questions.question: What is the first-line optical treatment for simple myopia?
questions.explanation: Divergent (concave) lenses correct myopia by dispersing light rays before they enter the eye, shifting the focal point back to coincide with the retina.

questions.question: What additional symptom can accompany blurred distance vision in myopia?
questions.explanation: Frontal headache is a common symptom in uncorrected myopia due to sustained visual effort and excessive contraction of the ciliary muscle to try to focus on distant objects.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of cycloplegia in autorefraction for myopia?
questions.explanation: Cycloplegia with drugs such as cyclopentolate or atropine inhibits the ciliary muscle, eliminating active accommodation, allowing the true refractive error to be measured without interference from the accommodative tone.

questions.question: In static retinoscopy, what movement of the fringes indicates uncorrected myopia?
questions.explanation: In retinoscopy, the 'against' motion indicates that the remote point is closer to the eye than the retinoscope, characteristic of uncorrected myopia; the 'with' movement suggests farsightedness or emmetropia.

questions.question: What lens is used to neutralize retinoscopic movement in a myopic eye?
questions.explanation: In retinoscopy, to neutralize the 'against' movement of a myopic eye, negative lenses (divergent) are added until the neutralization point is achieved, where the movement of the stripes disappears.

questions.question: What is the most commonly used cycloplegic agent in children for autorefraction?
questions.explanation: Cyclopentolate 1% is the cycloplegic of choice in children due to its rapid onset (30-60 min), moderate duration and adequate accommodative paralysis; Atropine is reserved for special cases due to its prolonged effect.

questions.question: Which finding on autorefraction under cycloplegia confirms true myopia?
questions.explanation: True myopia is confirmed when autorefraction under cycloplegia shows a negative value (spherical or spherical equivalent) that remains stable after accommodative paralysis, ruling out pseudomyopia due to accommodative spasm.

questions.question: What is the standard working distance in retinoscopy to compensate for examiner error?
questions.explanation: The usual working distance in retinoscopy is 67 cm (equivalent to 1.5 D), which is subtracted from the final result to obtain the patient's actual refraction; other distances such as 50 cm (2 D) are also used but less frequently.

questions.question: What type of lens is used to correct myopia?
questions.explanation: Myopia is corrected with divergent (concave) lenses, which shift the focus of the image backwards so that it falls on the retina.

questions.question: What is the dioptric sign of myopia lenses?
questions.explanation: Divergent lenses have negative dioptric power, which allows light rays to diverge and correct the excess convergence of the myopic eye.

questions.question: What shape are the lenses used to correct myopia?
questions.explanation: Biconcave lenses are divergent and are used to correct myopia, as they reduce excessive convergence of the ocular optical system.

questions.question: What optical effect does a diverging lens produce in the myopic eye?
questions.explanation: The diverging lens reduces excessive convergence of the myopic eye, moving the focal point towards the retina and improving distance vision.

questions.question: What is the main indication for divergent lenses in ophthalmology?
questions.explanation: Divergent (concave) lenses are mainly indicated for the correction of myopia, since they compensate for the excess refractive power of the eye.

questions.question: What characteristic does the image formed by a divergent lens have in a myopic eye?
questions.explanation: Divergent lenses produce a virtual, upright image, allowing the myopic eye to focus on distant objects by reducing excessive convergence.

questions.question: What is the main difference between LASIK and PRK in refractive surgery?
questions.explanation: In LASIK, a corneal flap is created that is lifted to apply the laser, while in PRK the epithelium is directly removed without a flap, which constitutes its main technical difference.

questions.question: Which postoperative complication is more characteristic of PRK than LASIK?
questions.explanation: Corneal haze (subepithelial opacity) is a more common complication after PRK due to the removal of the epithelium and the scar response, unlike LASIK where the risk of flap complications predominates.

questions.question: What corneal parameter is critical for selecting a LASIK candidate patient?
questions.explanation: Central corneal thickness is essential to determine whether there is sufficient safe residual stroma after ablation; insufficient thickness increases the risk of ectasia, being a fundamental exclusion criterion.

questions.question: Which of the following is a common adverse effect of LASIK surgery?
questions.explanation: Postoperative dry eye is a very common adverse effect after LASIK due to the section of corneal nerve fibers during flap creation, which alters innervation and tear production.

questions.question: How is LASEK different from conventional PRK?
questions.explanation: In LASEK, diluted alcohol is used to loosen and displace the epithelium like an epithelial flap, unlike PRK where it is removed mechanically, seeking to reduce pain and accelerate re-epithelialization.

questions.question: What condition absolutely contraindicates corneal refractive surgery?
questions.explanation: Advanced keratoconus is an absolute contraindication to any corneal refractive surgery because the cornea is already weakened and ectatic; additional ablation would worsen the disease and increase the risk of perforation.

questions.question: What is the most serious complication associated with high myopia?
questions.explanation: High myopia, especially degenerative myopia, predisposes to rhegmatogenous retinal detachment due to retinal thinning and the presence of retinal holes.

questions.question: What structure of the eye is weakened in high myopia, increasing the risk of retinal detachment?
questions.explanation: In high myopia, the axial elongation of the eyeball stretches and thins the peripheral retina, favoring the formation of retinal tears that can progress to detachment.

questions.question: What is the classic symptom of an incipient retinal detachment?
questions.explanation: The vision of floaters (myodesopsias) and light flashes (photopsias) are typical initial symptoms of retinal detachment, due to vitreous traction on the retina.

questions.question: What mechanical risk factor predisposes to retinal detachment in myopes?
questions.explanation: High myopia accelerates vitreous liquefaction and posterior vitreous detachment, which can produce retinal traction and tears, triggering detachment.

questions.question: What test is considered the test of choice to evaluate the risk of retinal detachment in high myopia?
options: Fundus of eye with pupillary dilation
questions.explanation: Dilated fundus allows examination of the peripheral retina for predisposing lesions such as holes, tears, or honeycomb degeneration, which indicate risk of detachment.

questions.question: What is the most common surgical treatment for rhegmatogenous retinal detachment?
questions.explanation: Pars plana vitrectomy with gas or silicone oil is the standard procedure to repair rhegmatogenous retinal detachments, eliminating vitreous traction and closing tears.

questions.question: What is the main cause of the increased susceptibility to open-angle glaucoma in high myopia?
questions.explanation: In high myopia, corneal thinning and increased globe deformability contribute to increased susceptibility to open-angle glaucoma, although intraocular pressure may be normal.

questions.question: What morphological finding of the optic nerve is characteristic in patients with high myopia and open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Temporal optic atrophy is a typical finding in high myopia, and in combination with open-angle glaucoma it can mask or simulate glaucomatous changes, making diagnosis difficult.

questions.question: What is the main diagnostic challenge in open angle glaucoma associated with high myopia?
questions.explanation: Open-angle glaucoma in high myopia often presents with normal intraocular pressure, so the main challenge is to differentiate it from normal tension glaucoma, which also occurs without ocular hypertension.

questions.question: What topical antiglaucomatous medication is the initial choice in patients with high myopia and open angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Latanoprost, a prostaglandin analogue, is the first-line treatment for open-angle glaucoma, including that associated with high myopia, due to its effectiveness in reducing intraocular pressure with a daily dose.

questions.question: Which optical coherence tomography (OCT) abnormality is most characteristic in high myopia with open-angle glaucoma?
options: Thinning of the nerve fiber layer
Thickening of the nerve fiber layer
questions.explanation: In high myopia, thinning of the retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) is characteristic, and in the presence of open-angle glaucoma this thinning is accentuated, being a crucial marker in OCT.

questions.question: What anatomical factor predisposes to the development of open-angle glaucoma in patients with high myopia?
questions.explanation: The increased axial length in high myopia stretches the eyeball, which can alter the architecture of the iridocorneal angle and optic nerve, increasing the risk of open-angle glaucoma.

questions.question: What is the main retinal complication in high myopia?
questions.explanation: In high myopia (>6 diopters) the axial elongation of the eyeball stretches the retina, favoring tears and rhegmatogenous detachment, a more frequent and serious complication.

questions.question: What funduscopic finding is characteristic of high myopia?
questions.explanation: Peripapillary choroideremia (chorioretinal atrophy around the optic disc) is typical due to stretching of the posterior pole in high myopia.

questions.question: What is the mechanical risk factor for retinal detachment in high myopia?
options: Chronic inflammation of the uveal tract
questions.explanation: Excessive axial elongation (>26 mm) stretches the retina and vitreous, producing retinal tears that allow subretinal fluid to enter.

questions.question: Which macular hole is most frequently associated with high myopia?
questions.explanation: The myopic macular hole (or macular hole in high myopia) is produced by stretching and thinning of the fovea, differentiating from the senile one due to its axial mechanism.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for choroidal neovascularization in high myopia?
questions.explanation: Intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, aflibercept) are first line for myopic choroidal neovascularization, with a better safety profile than photodynamic therapy.

questions.question: Which late retinal complication is most prevalent in patients with high myopia?
questions.explanation: Myopic retinoschisis (separation of the inner retinal layers) is a common late complication due to continuous stretching of the posterior pole in high myopia.

questions.question: What is the main environmental protective factor against the development of myopia in children?
questions.explanation: Exposure to natural light outdoors is associated with a lower incidence of childhood myopia, possibly due to greater release of retinal dopamine that inhibits axial growth of the eye.

questions.question: What mechanism is postulated for the protective effect of natural light against myopia?
questions.explanation: Natural light stimulates the release of dopamine in the retina, which inhibits the axial growth of the eyeball, reducing the risk of progressive myopia in children.

questions.question: How much daily time outdoors is recommended to prevent myopia in children?
questions.explanation: Studies suggest that at least 1-2 hours a day of outdoor exposure significantly reduce the incidence and progression of myopia in children.

questions.question: What type of outdoor activity is associated with greater protection against myopia?
questions.explanation: Outdoor sports involve distance vision and greater exposure to natural light, which maximizes the protective effect against the development of myopia in children.

questions.question: Which child population benefits most from outdoor exposure to prevent myopia?
options: Children with a family history of myopia
questions.explanation: Children with a family history of myopia are at higher genetic risk, and outdoor exposure is especially beneficial in delaying or preventing its onset.

questions.question: What is the effect of natural light on the retina that prevents myopia?
questions.explanation: Natural light increases the release of dopamine in the retina, a catecholamine that regulates eye growth and stops axial elongation associated with myopia.

questions.question: What is the standard screening method to detect myopia in schoolchildren?
questions.explanation: Refraction under cycloplegia is the reference method for screening for myopia in children and adolescents, since it eliminates accommodation and allows the real refractive error to be measured.

questions.question: At what age is it recommended to start refractive screening in asymptomatic children?
options: Only if there is a history
questions.explanation: It is recommended to start refractive screening before school entry (between 3 and 5 years) to detect myopia and other visual defects early, optimizing treatment.

questions.question: What refractive finding defines myopia in an adolescent?
options: Spherical equivalent less than or equal to -0.50 D
questions.explanation: Myopia is defined as a spherical equivalent less than or equal to -0.50 diopters, which indicates that the focal point forms in front of the retina, causing blurred distance vision.

questions.question: What is the most important modifiable risk factor for developing myopia in schoolchildren?
questions.explanation: Prolonged time spent in near vision activities (reading, devices) is a key modifiable risk factor for the development and progression of myopia in children and adolescents.
hashtags: Morgan I. et al. Prog Retin Eye Res 2021

questions.question: What complementary test is used to confirm the progression of myopia in adolescents?
questions.explanation: Ocular biometry measuring axial length is the standard test for documenting myopia progression, as axial increase directly correlates with refractive error progression.

questions.question: What is the first-line intervention to slow the progression of myopia in schoolchildren?
questions.explanation: Topical atropine in low doses (0.01%) is the first-line pharmacological intervention to slow the progression of myopia in children and adolescents, with demonstrated efficacy and minimal adverse effects.
